did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the InceptionV3 deep learning model (a convolutional neural network) for skin disease classification. It utilizes the DermNet dataset for image categorization and reports achieving an accuracy higher than 78.8%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using the InceptionV3 model on the DermNet dataset for skin disease classification. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing, transfer learning, and model architecture, and reports specific performance metrics (accuracy > 78.8%). Although published in a conference proceedings (AIDE), it contains new results rather than just summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin disease classification using inceptionV3 model
journal: International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of skin diseases such as psoriasis, acne, and eczema using the InceptionV3 deep learning model. The researchers utilized the DermNet dataset, which contains over 20,000 images of various skin conditions, to train and evaluate the convolutional neural network. The methodology involved transfer learning, where the pre-trained InceptionV3 architecture was fine-tuned on the DermNet data. The dataset was split into an 80% training set and a 20% testing set to assess model performance. The primary evaluation metric reported was accuracy, with the model achieving a result higher than 78.8%. The study demonstrates the effectiveness of applying contemporary deep learning techniques to automate skin disease categorization.

The paper mentions specific subtypes (plaque, guttate, inverse, pustular, erythrodermic) in the context of related literature (Aijaz et al.) and lists psoriasis as a target category for the model's testing dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin disease categories (psoriasis, acne, eczema)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'Skin disease classification' using the InceptionV3 model. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for skin illnesses such as psoriasis, acne, and eczema using the DermNet dataset. Performance is measured by accuracy (higher than 78.8%), which is a metric for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: DermNet
datasets_used.description: A freely accessible dataset containing pictures of various skin conditions. The paper notes it contains more than 20,000 photos representing various categories of skin disorders, but specifies that around 4,454 images were used in this study.

use.training: Used for training the InceptionV3 model (80% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (20% of the dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

DermNet.notes: The paper states that on the DermNet dataset, the InceptionV3 model achieved higher than 78.8% accuracy. The dataset consisted of 4454 images, split 80% for training and 20% for testing. The model was also evaluated on a test set of 974 images for three classes (psoriasis, acne, eczema). The average precision was 0.34 and the average recall was 0.36. Specific per-class metrics were: Acne (Precision: 0.32, Recall: 0.32, F1: 0.32), Psoriasis (Precision: 0.35, Recall: 0.32, F1: 0.34), Eczema (Precision: 0.36, Recall: 0.38, F1: 0.36). The paper also notes that ResNet50 was examined but yielded less accurate results than InceptionV3.

OLE.notes: The paper reports top-1 accuracy of 31.1% and top-5 accuracy of 69% on the OLE dataset. Note: The context text contains OCR artifacts ('3 1 . 1%' and '69%'), which are interpreted as 31.1% and 69% respectively based on standard accuracy reporting formats and the preceding '73.1%' and '91%' for DermNet which also had OCR artifacts ('73.1%' likely 73.1% or similar, but the text says '73.1%' and '91%' for DermNet in one chunk and '78.8%' in another. The specific OLE values are explicitly linked to the OLE dataset in the text: 'top-1 and top-5 accuracies for the test on the OLE dataset were 31.1% and 69%, respectively.' The OCR '3 1 . 1%' is interpreted as 31.1%.

components: Pooling layers (Average pooling and Maximal pooling)

preprocessing_techniques: Image scaling to normalize dimensions (resized to have the same dimensions for deep learning models)
conclusions: The InceptionV3 deep learning model was employed to assist users in evaluating skin diseases such as psoriasis, acne, and eczema, which are difficult to discern for individuals without a medical background.
The study demonstrated that the InceptionV3 model can achieve higher than 78.8% accuracy on the DermNet dataset.
On the DermNet dataset, the model achieved a top-1 accuracy of 73.1% and a top-5 accuracy of 91%.
On the OLE dataset, the model achieved a top-1 accuracy of 31.1% and a top-5 accuracy of 69%.
The DermNet dataset was utilized for image categorization in the model.
The study highlights the potential of applying contemporary deep learning techniques to skin disease classification tasks.
important_remarks: The study focuses only on three skin conditions (psoriasis, acne, eczema), excluding critical malignancies like melanoma or basal cell carcinoma, limiting clinical utility for serious dermatological screening.

The reported accuracy (>78.8%) is significantly higher than the detailed per-class metrics provided (average precision 0.34, average recall 0.36), suggesting potential class imbalance or reporting inconsistencies that obscure true model performance.
The model was evaluated on the OLE dataset with very low performance (top-1 accuracy 31.1%), indicating poor generalizability or significant domain shift when applied to external data.
The dataset size used for training and testing was small (4,454 images total), which may lead to overfitting and limits the robustness of the deep learning model.
No external validation on independent clinical datasets was performed; the study relies solely on the DermNet dataset, raising concerns about real-world applicability.
The paper does not report standard statistical significance tests or confidence intervals for the reported metrics, weakening the evidence for the model's superiority.
The study compares InceptionV3 only against ResNet50 (which performed worse) and does not benchmark against state-of-the-art models or human dermatologists in the same experimental setup.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; the paper does not detail how images were split to ensure no patient overlap between training and testing sets, which is critical in medical imaging.
